Now that we've discussed a little bit about bringing a client on, you know, and what skills they require to meet your expectations, I want you to think about the typical way client onboarding is done in this sort of fitness setting, right? The very beginning, people are slammed, slammed with an oddly specific plan tailored to them with none of the skills, none of the skills to implement it. And this creates a lot of stress for the client and a ton of work for the practitioner. So let's get real here. In a typical situation, there is no onboarding process at all. At best, a well-meaning practitioner will work themselves to death dealing with emails, phone calls, voice messages, video conferences, and texts by confused and frustrated clients. That basically means your client was just thrown to the wolves and now you have to pull them back out. Okay? There may be a few cheat sheets that get handed out, but those are typically expected to solve everything from the very beginning. And then when a client comes with questions, you get insulted. So let's just put that sort of mentality aside, shall we? Without properly onboarding a client, the client will get frustrated and bail. They will end up taking longer than what the beginning phase should be to get their act together. Okay? And it will be an unnecessarily exhausting process for the practitioner and the client, for you and the client. A client that really cares will most likely bother the living crap out of you. Right? And the morally upright practitioner will put an unreasonable amount of front-end, high-stress work in unnecessarily. So even if the practitioner has a best interest in mind, a lot of people do not onboard the clients properly and have a crap ton of work to do in the beginning, work that they could front-load in a different way that's less stressful for everyone. Okay? So onboarding the client during the first few weeks of the program and having the program dedicated to that onboarding process is very important. Okay? This is how you stack the cards in the practitioner's favor, in your favor. This is where the practitioner strategically exposes the client to philosophies, tools, and skills they need to be successful in the program. So that means every week or so or every few days or however you decide to structure the program, something new is implemented, a new behavior, and a new skill that a person can practice without overloading people and expecting them to be perfect right out of the gate. Okay? The client can familiarize themselves with all the information without having to immediately implement it to let things soak in. They can't be exacting from the very beginning. And that's why the whole process is introduced to new clients the way it is, where they have very minimum amount of behaviors that they need to execute, and as time goes on, they are fed a little bit more responsibility, and as they master those new skills, they get freedom in the process. In the very beginning, like I said, the client doesn't have very many marching orders. So this is the time to be developing the relationship with the client in such a way that when you do start to give them more skills and activities and behaviors that they need to perform, they are comfortable with taking those things on. One, because they have some idea that they're coming, and two, because they trust you to implement them. The first few weeks of any program could be considered a diagnostic phase, and this is where really the client is collecting data and giving that data to you, whether it's through things that they are physically measuring, through messages, emails, whatever, and then that information over the next few weeks is what you use to figure out on the back end how to tailor your plan to a client. This is how you know what things they need to be exposed to so you can nip the behaviors they need to learn in the bud at the very beginning. This is essentially how to help them be a good client. Expecting everyone to be a good client in the beginning is ridiculous, and so by giving very minimum specifications during the first few weeks of the client being part of your program, you can use that time to help the client learn the skills they need to learn to be successful, which those skills have already been discussed in an earlier video, but this is how all that stuff ends up tying together. If you're finding the minimum education requirements for your program that exceed the length of the first couple of weeks, then there are a few options here. You need to extend the length of time in your brain that they need to learn material, like you're trying to force too much down their throat at once, or two, the program requirements need to change, meaning maybe you're feeding them too much and that extra material might be better suited for an advanced program, or the extra material may be better suited for conversations down the line, maybe a few months later after the basics have been mastered. Quite often, practitioners, they get a hellacious amount of questions about a processor skill they wish for the client to implement from the client. If you follow the guidelines in the curriculum development section, like what exactly does a client need to succeed, what resources do I need to provide for them to make sure that happens, then it may appear that, okay, maybe I'm just missing stuff that I need to provide the client in the beginning. Take a step back, it's possible that maybe you're just overloading them, honestly, keeping it simple, stupid is best. I know that other guys have covered that material a little more in depth, but I'm going to reiterate here, the least amount of material that you give them to be as successful as possible is the key. Every so often, it's worth it to audit your onboarding process and ask yourself, does the client really need to learn this, or did I just really want to include it? Like every once in a while, every few months, or every few clients, take a step back and see how you interact with clients for the first few weeks and ask yourself that question. The whole purpose of this lesson is to communicate that it is unethical to have the expectation of a client to execute a plan with inappropriate knowledge and inappropriate skills to execute it. Okay, the onboarding process, meaning how you communicate with them in the first few weeks and the order in which you provide them information to execute, which is already planned out for you, it gives the novice client a fighting chance at succeeding without unnecessary discouragement. Okay, when implemented properly, the full onboarding process takes a massive load off the practitioner. Hell, it's a systemic way of creating the ideal client. How could it not? Okay, in other words, a polished onboarding process puts the practitioner in a position to practice and always operate with the client in the forefront of your mind. All right, and all this stuff's planned out for you in the curriculum that I laid out. So it's all good. All you got to do is plug and play with your own flavor.
